## [00:00 - 03:01]

Alright guys, so once you have essentially validated a funnel for the client for the account, meaning you're getting sales and everything is working really well, it's time to optimize what you're doing. And one of the ways to do that is to build what we call a long tail funnel. Now there's a lot of different terms out there for a long tail funnel. You may also refer to it sometimes as an evergreen funnel, but essentially what it is, is it's building out the retargeting sequences and segmenting out the different types of audiences that are so warm to hot audiences and splitting them out a bit more granularly so that we can communicate to them based on their specific needs. And the reason for that is if you think about it, what you did in the initial testing phase and minimum viable funnel, and maybe you even added a video at the top of the funnel, your retargeting is pretty basic at this stage, right? It's a 14 day add to cart or view content retargeting, correct? So and maybe you've got 14 days or 30 days, so it's pretty broad and it's pretty basic. Now what we want to do is actually separate that out a little bit better so that we're not only retargeting people that added to cart or viewed content, but also retargeting our customers to try to get more repeat purchases. And also retargeting people based on specific pages that they viewed on the website such as shipping or sizing FAQs, etc, etc. And what this does is it really, really optimizes that funnel. It ensures that you're not leaving any money on the table. So some of the typical audiences that you'll see, and I just want to do a very big disclaimer here guys that this is kind of a night, these numbers here is kind of a guideline. But how much you segment your audiences down and what I mean by that is for example, if you look at view content in the last five days, view content in the last 10 days, those would be two different ad sets, right? It's two different audiences. Now if you have a very, very small amount of people visiting your website, so those audiences are very small, i.e. they're under about 300 people, I would say that you are better off keeping that, you know, view content five days and 10 days together. So this is a generalization and in what you're seeing here is probably best suited to a business or a website that does get quite a bit of traffic. So there's big audiences size here, there's a big audience size and the retargeting will feed out properly. So for example, what we're doing here is we're actually going to segment out people based on when they viewed the product. I'll take you into some creative ideas in a moment. Add to Cart should actually be before purchase here, I don't know why I did that in that

order, so let's just go and swipe that in here really quickly. Add to Cart, again you're segmenting that out, add to Cart in the last five days, add to Cart in the last 10 days, add to Cart in the last 30 days, and then purchased. So for example, for purchased, people that purchased, well what you can do is you can essentially create frequencies with your ads. So if we go inside the ad account here and under our audiences where we can create our custom audiences, and we select purchase here, and you can do this for example in the last 30 days or 60 days, and we can go by frequency, so aggregated value. Frequency is greater than or equal to two. So this is telling Facebook anyone that triggered purchase more than twice in the last 30 days or 60 days, and then you would essentially name this times two 60 days for example. So you can create segments to essentially reward people that purchase multiple times from you, or even exclude people that purchase multiple times. So for example, you could create an ad set where you're retargeting people that purchased 60 days ago, but exclude those that purchased again within the last 60 days. So let's say you're selling razors or something, right? So if they purchased again, you know they're probably going to be okay, they're not going to buy just yet, but what you want to do is you want to encourage those that had already purchased to repurchase from you, but exclude those that purchased again. So this is kind of the stuff that you can get into. It's very granular, but it has incredible ROAs on it, our return on ad spend. You can also reward your existing customers and create loyalty, build ambassadors out of them. So for example here, this one is just creating an ad set of people that purchased and are about to have an anniversary in the next 30 days saying, hey, happy birthday. So below what you'll see here is kind of an example of how you would set that up, that custom audience. For example, if you're trying to target people that purchased 30 to 60 days ago, and the reason you would want to make it 30 to 60 is if they only purchased two weeks ago, they may not even have received the product yet, or they may not have had a chance to consume it. ## [09:04 - 11:27]

But one of the things that you can do when you retarget people that abandon add to cart is call them out. Say, come back. You left something in your cart. Amazon is really good at doing this. You can also give them an offer here. And if someone added to cart in the last 30 to 60 days, but still didn't purchase, what do you need to do to get them over the line? Again, testimonials or maybe a different offer. So start to think about some of those things there as well. Now another way to build out your long tail funnel as well is to think about specific pages on that website. So typically in e-commerce, you're looking at things like shipping, about us, FAQs. These people are trying to figure out if this is the right fit for them or they have questions and for whatever reason they didn't purchase. So it's a really cool idea as you'll see in this example here. This could be retargeting people that viewed an FAQ of what is this thing? What does it taste like? And retargeting them with maybe a video or information about that. Basically addressing head on those potential questions. So for example, if someone's checking out the shipping page, well you could retarget them. So people that checked out shipping but didn't purchase, you could retarget them and say, we have free 30 day returns. So it's very little risk for you. Just put out that hook, right?
